1.description: Leverage target and adjustment speed. The figure reports results from 16-quarter rolling regressions estimating the dynamic adjustment model in two steps. Step 1 models leveragebit+1as a function of explanatory variableszitand passive leveragebit+1p,bit+1=Ōē‚ä§zit+Ōąbit+1p+őĶit+1. Step 2 models the adjustment speed(bit+1‚ąíbit+1p)/(bŐāit‚ąó‚ąíbit+1p)as a function of explanatory variableswit,bit+1‚ąíbit+1p=ő≥‚ä§[wit√ó(bŐāit‚ąó‚ąíbit+1p)]+őĹit+1, withbŐāit‚ąó=(ŌēŐā/(1‚ąíŌąŐā))‚ä§zit, where the estimatesŌēŐāandŌąŐācome from step 1. The left panel shows the average actual leverage and the average estimated leverage target from step 1, using the leverage target predicted for the last periodt=16of each window. The right panel shows the adjustment speedőĽŐāestimated in step 1 and the average adjustment speed estimated in step 2, using the bank-level average predicted adjustment speed116‚ąĎt=116ő≥‚ä§witwithin each window. The sample consists of quarterly data from 1996Q1 to 2016Q4 and includes any bank that is ever in the top 500 by assets in a given sample quarter. 2.description: Aggregate vulnerability index and decomposition into factors. The figure shows the aggregate vulnerability index (left panel) and the decomposition into multiplicative factors based on equation (8) (right panel). All series are normalized to 100 at the beginning of the sample. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Fire-sale externality of most systemic banks and assets. The figure shows the most systemic banks and assets, plotting the evolution of the measuresSBitandSAktfrom equations (9) and (10), respectively. Which banks and assets are most systemic is determined by sample averages. Series are normalized to sum to 100 at the beginning of the sample. ‚ÄúRRE‚ÄĚ is residential real estate, ‚ÄúC¬†&¬†I‚ÄĚ is commercial and industrial, ‚ÄúRepo & FF loans‚ÄĚ is federal funds sold and securities purchased under agreements to resell, and ‚ÄúCRE‚ÄĚ is commercial real estate. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Effect of factors on AV. The left panel shows the contribution of the four factors in equation (8) to the variance of log AV, using the variance decomposition given by equation (18). The right panel shows the ratio of actual AV to a counterfactual AV in which all banks are homogeneous. ‚ÄúPre-crisis‚ÄĚ is 1999Q3 to 2006Q4, ‚ÄúCrisis‚ÄĚ is 2007Q1 to 2009Q4, and ‚ÄúPostcrisis‚ÄĚ is 2010Q1 to 2016Q3. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Effects of TARP, capital regulation, and liquidity regulation on AV. The figure shows the ratio of benchmark AV to a counterfactual AV in which each bank's leverage is adjusted by the capital injection it received through TARP (left panel), a counterfactual AV in which, starting in 2008Q4, each bank's leverage is kept constant at its 2006Q4 level (middle panel), and a counterfactual AV in which, starting in 2008Q4, each bank's asset portfolio weights are kept constant at their 2006Q4 levels (right panel). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Comparison of AVto other systemic risk measures leading up to the crisis. The figure plots time-series evolution of AV (solid line) and 35 systemic risk measures (dashed lines) surveyed in Bisias et¬†al. (2012) and Giglio, Kelly, and Pruitt (2016) between 2003Q1 and 2009Q1. All measures are normalized to 100 in 2003Q1 and shown in log-scale, winsorized for values lower than 50 and higher than 350. The top left panel compares AV to the 22 measures that give late warning of systemic risk, starting to rise in mid-2007 or later. The bottom left panel shows AV and the nine measures that provide neither a clear trend nor any discernible signal of a crisis. The middle and right panels compare AV to each of the four systemic risk measures that have an increasing precrisis trend similar to the one that AV displays. Internet Appendix SectionVIIIprovides plots identifying each measure as well as details on how they were constructed. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
